summary: Gruber responds to Apple's public statement addressing the iPhone battery throttling controversy. He praises Apple's response for clearly explaining the performance management feature and announcing concrete remedies: a reduced $29 battery replacement price and an upcoming iOS update showing battery health. He argues the controversy was entirely self-inflicted by Apple's failure to communicate proactively, noting that when Apple does communicate, they do so effectively and honestly.
key_points: Apple's $29 battery replacement program (down from $79) is a strong concrete response to customer concerns
The upcoming iOS 11 update should show battery health status as soon as performance throttling kicks in, not only when the battery is in dire condition
The entire controversy was caused by Apple's failure to communicate, not by the throttling feature itself
Apple's communication problems follow a consistent pattern: silence creates space for accusations of bad intent
money_quotes: The funny thing about Apple is that their communication problems tend to happen only when they don't communicate at all.
This whole iPhone battery controversy erupted only because Apple had never explained what was going on, which opened them up to accusations of nefarious intent.
When they do communicate, they do so with clarity, plain language, and honesty. And, when called for — as in this case — humility.
This is a terrific response, both in terms of explaining what has actually been going on, and in terms of the steps they're taking going forward.
Reducing the price of authorized battery replacements to $29 is really great.
key_insight: Apple's battery throttling controversy was not a product problem but a communication problem — their silence invited suspicion that transparency would have prevented.
